title: Requests, offers, and delivery tracked in spreadsheets and email

seo.title: Requests, offers, and delivery tracked in spreadsheets and email
seo.description: I help companies where incoming requests, offers, and delivery work are managed across spreadsheets, email, and manual follow-up, and the process is becoming...

page.title: Requests, offers, and delivery tracked in spreadsheets and email
page.h1: Requests, offers, and delivery outgrow spreadsheets and email
page.description: I help companies where incoming requests, offers, and delivery work are managed across spreadsheets, email, and manual follow-up, and the process is becoming hard to control reliably.
page.primaryQuery: job tracking in spreadsheets and email

hero.title: Requests and jobs are being held together by spreadsheets, email, and people’s memory
hero.subtitle: A common situation where the company still functions, but each additional job increases chaos, error risk, and manual coordination pressure.

intro: Spreadsheets and email are a natural starting point. The problem starts when the same job involves more people, more statuses, and more handoffs between steps.
At that point it becomes unclear where the current information lives, who owns the next step, which offer is valid, and what stage delivery is actually in.

sections.title: Typical symptoms
body: When the process depends on spreadsheets and manual coordination for too long, the cost is not only admin effort. Error risk, delays, and lost information between sales and delivery also grow.
bullets: incoming requests are not tracked in one reliable place
several offer versions exist without clear continuity
sales and delivery hold different views of the same job
job status is discovered through email, calls, or asking colleagues
ownership of the next step is unclear

sections.title: How I approach it
body: The first step is to identify where the process loses visibility and where the biggest friction appears. Only then does it make sense to decide what should stay simple and what already needs an internal system.

sections.title: What a good outcome looks like
body: The goal is not a quick patch. The goal is to restore control, confidence, and a practical next step.
bullets: clearer view of where the process is breaking down
better judgement on what needs system support and what does not yet
less dependence on manual information lookup
a stronger foundation for a system connecting requests, offers, and delivery

faq.question: Is the spreadsheet itself the problem?
faq.answer: No. The issue starts when the spreadsheet tries to act as workflow, accountability layer, and cross-team handoff system at the same time.

faq.question: Can we start with one part of the process?
faq.answer: Yes. It is often smarter to begin where the current chaos or operating loss is the highest.

faq.question: Do we need a full new system immediately?
faq.answer: No. Sometimes the best first step is to clarify the process and then scope the first internal-tool phase around the weakest point.

for: companies with project-based sales and follow-up delivery
teams that can no longer keep control through spreadsheets and email
situations needing better continuity between sales and execution
notFor: very simple one-stage processes with no handoffs
marketing-only lead capture with no delivery work behind it
teams looking only for a new spreadsheet template instead of workflow support

cta.label: Explore the project fit
cta.note: A short summary of the current state and the main risk is enough to start.
